h1Title: Chronic Pancreatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic pancreatitis is a progressive, irreversible inflammatory disease of the pancreas characterized by fibrosis, ductal distortion, and destruction of both exocrine and endocrine parenchyma. It affects approximately 50 per 100,000 adults in the United States, with alcohol as the leading identifiable cause in Western populations, accounting for 45-70% of cases. The hallmark is recurrent or persistent upper abdominal pain, typically severe, boring in quality, and radiating to the back; malabsorption and diabetes develop as functional tissue is progressively lost. Untreated pain drives opioid dependence in 30-50% of patients. Modern management integrates pain control, pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, endoscopic or surgical drainage procedures, and lifestyle modification.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 50 per 100,000 adults in the US; rising incidence over the past two decades (GBD 2017 data)
keyStats.demographics: Alcohol-related chronic pancreatitis predominantly affects men aged 35-55; idiopathic forms affect women more equally; tropical calcific pancreatitis peaks in young adults aged 15-40 in South Asia
keyStats.avgAge: Alcoholic chronic pancreatitis typically presents at age 35-45 after 10-20 years of heavy drinking; symptom onset to diagnosis averages 4-6 years
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated global prevalence 50-80 per 100,000; approximately 400,000 new cases diagnosed globally each year (GBD 2017)

definition: Chronic pancreatitis (ICD-10: K86.0 for alcohol-induced; K86.1 for other forms) is defined as persistent pancreatic inflammation leading to irreversible structural changes including parenchymal fibrosis, ductal strictures and dilatation, and calcifications. Unlike acute pancreatitis, where the gland may recover completely, chronic pancreatitis produces permanent damage that progresses even after the causative insult is removed. Two major functional consequences follow: exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI), resulting in maldigestion of fat and protein with steatorrhoea and weight loss, typically manifesting when 90% or more of exocrine function is lost; and endocrine insufficiency — type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes mellitus — due to destruction of islet cell tissue, developing in 30-50% of patients within 10-20 years of disease onset. The predominant symptom is abdominal pain, which may be episodic early in the course or chronic and unrelenting as the disease advances. Alcohol consumption is the most common modifiable cause, though genetic mutations (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR), autoimmune pancreatitis, obstructive causes, and idiopathic disease account for a substantial proportion. Management is coordinated by gastroenterology, endocrinology, nutrition, pain medicine, and upper GI surgery.

typesClassification.description: Most common form in high-income countries. Requires sustained heavy alcohol use (typically >5 units/day for >5 years) combined with genetic susceptibility factors. Pancreatic calcifications on imaging are characteristic. Continued drinking accelerates progression and substantially worsens pain.

No identifiable cause found; accounts for 20-30% of cases. Two subtypes: early-onset (onset age 15-25, mostly male, predominantly pain) and late-onset (age 50-60, equal sex, often exocrine insufficiency predominant with few calcifications).

PRSS1 gain-of-function mutations cause autosomal-dominant hereditary pancreatitis with onset in childhood; SPINK1 and CFTR mutations act as susceptibility modifiers. Genetic testing is recommended in unexplained chronic or recurrent acute pancreatitis, especially in young patients and those with family history.

Type 1 AIP is IgG4-related disease mimicking pancreatic cancer with a sausage-shaped pancreas on imaging, bile duct strictures, and dramatic steroid response. Type 2 AIP is associated with inflammatory bowel disease. Both types must be distinguished from carcinoma before empirical steroids are given.
Caused by sustained obstruction of the main pancreatic duct from pancreatic cancer, divisum, or post-traumatic stricture. Unlike other forms, may partially reverse if obstruction is relieved early.

primarySymptoms: Persistent or episodic upper abdominal pain, often severe and boring in quality, located in the epigastrium or left upper quadrant and radiating straight to the mid-back — present in over 85% of patients with alcoholic and idiopathic chronic pancreatitis.
Pain that worsens 15-30 minutes after eating (postprandial pain), leading patients to fear meals and reduce food intake, contributing to progressive weight loss.

Fatty, oily, foul-smelling stools (steatorrhoea) that are difficult to flush, occurring when pancreatic lipase output falls below 10% of normal — indicating advanced exocrine pancreatic insufficiency.
Significant unintentional weight loss from a combination of malabsorption, anorexia from pain, and fear-of-eating behavior; weight loss of 10-20% of body weight is common at late presentation.
Nausea and vomiting, particularly during pain episodes or in the presence of gastric outlet obstruction from a pseudocyst or adjacent inflammation.
New-onset or worsening diabetes mellitus (pancreatogenic type 3c diabetes) presenting with polyuria, polydipsia, and fatigue — develops in 30-50% within 10-20 years of onset.
Jaundice from common bile duct obstruction by fibrotic head-of-pancreas tissue or a pseudocyst — occurs in approximately 10-15% of patients.
Distended, tender epigastric mass from a pancreatic pseudocyst compressing adjacent structures.
Bloating, flatulence, and diarrhea from bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine promoted by impaired pancreatic secretion.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent attacks of acute pancreatitis (two or more episodes) in a patient with heavy alcohol use — each episode of acute pancreatitis is a potential stepping-stone to chronic disease.
Epigastric pain radiating to the back in a patient with daily alcohol use exceeding 5 units, even without elevated amylase or lipase on blood testing.
Unexplained fat malabsorption or vitamin deficiencies (particularly fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K) in a patient with upper abdominal symptoms.
Unexplained new-onset diabetes mellitus in a patient with a history of recurrent acute pancreatitis or heavy alcohol use — pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes warrants pancreatic imaging.
emergencySigns: Acute exacerbation with severe epigastric pain, vomiting, and rising lipase above three times the upper limit of normal — requires hospital admission for pain management, IV fluids, and evaluation for complications.
Fever, rigors, and rapid clinical deterioration in a patient with a known pseudocyst — suggests infected pseudocyst (pancreatic abscess) requiring urgent drainage.
Sudden onset of haematemesis or melena from gastric or duodenal varices in splenic vein thrombosis — a vascular complication of chronic pancreatitis requiring endoscopic or radiological management.
Hypoglycaemic episode in pancreatogenic diabetes — brittle hypoglycaemia from concomitant loss of glucagon-secreting alpha cells makes these patients at particular risk.

causes.description: Alcohol is the leading identifiable cause, responsible for 45-70% of chronic pancreatitis in Western populations. The threshold for risk is typically more than 4-5 standard drinks per day for 5 or more years, although genetic susceptibility determines why only 5-10% of heavy drinkers develop chronic pancreatitis. Alcohol and its metabolite acetaldehyde sensitize acinar cells to premature trypsin activation and trigger oxidative stress and stellate cell activation leading to fibrosis.

PRSS1 gain-of-function mutations cause autosomal-dominant hereditary pancreatitis by increasing trypsin stability and promoting auto-activation. SPINK1 and CFTR mutations act as susceptibility alleles that amplify risk from environmental triggers such as alcohol and smoking. Genetic causes account for approximately 20-30% of cases overall and a higher proportion in young patients.

Smoking is now established as an independent cause of chronic pancreatitis, not merely a cofactor with alcohol. Current smokers have approximately 2.5-3× the risk of non-smokers independent of alcohol use. Smoking accelerates progression, increases calcification rates, and raises the risk of pancreatic cancer. Cessation slows but does not halt disease progression.

IgG4-related (Type 1) and IBD-associated (Type 2) autoimmune pancreatitis cause diffuse pancreatic inflammation that can progress to fibrosis if untreated. Type 1 often mimics pancreatic cancer with a proximal stricture and mass effect; it responds to oral prednisolone 0.6-1 mg/kg/day, distinguishing it from malignancy.

Pancreatic duct obstruction from ductal calculi, strictures, pancreas divisum, or post-traumatic stenosis causes upstream pressure injury. Pancreas divisum — where the dorsal and ventral ducts fail to fuse during development, occurring in 5-10% of the population — is a contested risk factor that may predispose to chronic pancreatitis when combined with other genetic or environmental factors.

Severe hypertriglyceridaemia (above 1,000 mg/dL) causes acute pancreatitis through fatty acid injury to acinar cells; recurrent episodes lead to chronic changes. Hypercalcaemia from hyperparathyroidism can cause ductal calcification and chronic pancreatitis via intraductal calcium precipitation.

riskFactors.factor: Heavy alcohol use (>5 units/day for >5 years)
riskFactors.description: Risk scales with alcohol dose and duration; binge drinking may be less harmful than sustained daily heavy drinking, though both are injurious. Only 5-10% of heavy drinkers develop chronic pancreatitis, implicating genetic co-factors. Cessation slows progression but does not reverse existing fibrosis.

Independent of alcohol, smoking confers a 2.5-3× increased risk. Smoking and alcohol have synergistic effects — a smoker who drinks heavily has approximately 5-8× the risk of a non-smoking non-drinker. Cessation at any stage reduces the rate of calcification and pain progression.

riskFactors.factor: PRSS1, SPINK1, or CFTR gene mutations
riskFactors.description: PRSS1 mutations cause hereditary pancreatitis with 80% penetrance; SPINK1 and CFTR variants substantially amplify risk from environmental exposures. Genetic testing with a pancreatitis gene panel is indicated in young patients (under 35) and those with family history.

Two or more episodes of acute pancreatitis increase the risk of chronic pancreatitis by approximately 10-fold compared with the general population. Sentinel acute pancreatitis followed by recurrent bouts (recurrent acute pancreatitis) is a recognized pathway to chronic disease.

IgG4-related disease affecting other organs (salivary glands, bile ducts, kidneys, orbits) increases the probability of autoimmune pancreatitis. The diagnosis is often made on a combination of imaging, IgG4 serology, and response to steroids.

riskFactors.factor: Tropical regions and dietary cassava intake
riskFactors.description: Tropical calcific pancreatitis — prevalent in South India, sub-Saharan Africa, and Brazil — affects young, non-alcoholic, malnourished individuals. Dietary cyanogen from cassava combined with protein malnutrition and possibly SPINK1 mutations are implicated in the etiology.

affectedDemographics: Alcoholic chronic pancreatitis predominantly affects men aged 35-55 in high-income countries; male-to-female ratio approximately 5:1 in alcohol-related disease.
Idiopathic early-onset chronic pancreatitis affects young adults aged 15-30, predominantly male, across all ethnicities; no alcohol trigger.
Tropical calcific pancreatitis affects young, thin, non-drinking individuals in South India, Nigeria, and Brazil — a distinct epidemiology from Western disease.
Hereditary pancreatitis caused by PRSS1 mutations typically presents in childhood or adolescence and carries a 40-fold elevated lifetime risk of pancreatic cancer.
Autoimmune pancreatitis Type 1 is most common in men over age 50 from Japan and other East Asian countries, where IgG4-related disease is particularly prevalent.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis integrates clinical history, functional testing, and imaging. A diagnosis of chronic pancreatitis should be considered in any patient with recurrent or persistent upper abdominal pain radiating to the back, particularly with a history of heavy alcohol use, prior acute pancreatitis episodes, or relevant family history. Serum amylase and lipase may be normal or only mildly elevated in established chronic pancreatitis because fibrosis reduces functional acinar mass. CT of the abdomen with contrast remains the first imaging test of choice, demonstrating pancreatic calcifications (pathognomonic), ductal dilatation, parenchymal atrophy, and pseudocysts. MRI with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) provides superior soft-tissue detail and ductal imaging without radiation, and is preferred for younger patients and for planning endoscopic or surgical intervention. Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) detects early ductal and parenchymal changes not seen on CT and allows guided tissue sampling to exclude malignancy. Exocrine insufficiency is confirmed by a fecal elastase-1 concentration below 200 µg/g stool — a non-invasive, cost-effective test sensitive for moderate-to-severe EPI; direct pancreatic function testing (secretin stimulation) is more sensitive for early disease but only available at specialized centers. Endocrine function should be formally assessed with HbA1c and fasting glucose in all patients. Genetic testing (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR) is warranted in young patients or those without an obvious cause. ERCP is no longer recommended as a primary diagnostic tool but remains the procedure of choice for decompressing dilated ducts.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging; identifies pancreatic calcifications (pathognomonic), ductal dilatation, parenchymal atrophy, and pseudocysts
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-minute scan with IV contrast via an arm vein. Radiologist grades calcifications, duct width, and parenchymal changes. Available within hours. Calcifications visible on plain CT without contrast are diagnostic for chronic pancreatitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Superior ductal imaging without radiation; shows strictures, stones, and side-branch changes; preferred for pre-intervention planning and young patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 40-60 minutes in the MRI scanner, with or without IV contrast. No radiation. After secretin injection (secretin-MRCP), ductal dynamics can be assessed. Reports available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects early parenchymal changes (lobularity, stranding, calcifications) and ductal features; allows guided biopsy to exclude pancreatic cancer
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Done under sedation; a scope with an ultrasound transducer is passed into the stomach and duodenum. The pancreas is imaged from millimeters away. Procedure takes 30-45 minutes; recovery 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; concentration below 200 µg/g stool confirms moderate-to-severe EPI
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A random stool sample sent to the lab. No bowel preparation required. Results in 1-3 days. Levels above 200 µg/g are normal; below 100 µg/g indicates severe EPI. False-low results occur with watery stool.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes caused by destruction of islet cells
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws. HbA1c above 6.5% or fasting glucose above 7 mmol/L confirms diabetes. All patients with chronic pancreatitis should have annual glucose monitoring.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for autoimmune pancreatitis Type 1 (IgG4-related disease); levels above four times the upper limit of normal are highly specific
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw. Result in 24-72 hours. Elevated IgG4 combined with typical imaging triggers steroid trial under close supervision. IgG4 is elevated in 60-70% of Type 1 AIP cases.

treatmentOverview: Management of chronic pancreatitis addresses four distinct problems: pain control, exocrine insufficiency, endocrine insufficiency, and prevention of complications. The 2020 American College of Gastroenterology Clinical Practice Guidelines and the 2017 European Society of Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ESGE) guidelines form the evidence base. Alcohol and smoking cessation are the highest-priority interventions — both reduce the rate of acute exacerbations, slow calcification progression, and improve pain intensity. Analgesic therapy follows a stepwise approach: non-opioid first-line (paracetamol/NSAIDs), adjuvant neuromodulators (gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine), opioids as third-line with specialist oversight, and referral to pain medicine for refractory cases. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with high-dose enteric-coated microspheres (lipase 40,000-80,000 units with every main meal and 20,000-40,000 units with snacks) corrects fat malabsorption and improves weight and nutrition. Proton-pump inhibitors are co-prescribed because gastric acid inactivates supplemental enzymes if not inhibited. Endoscopic therapy (ERCP with stricture dilation, stone fragmentation with extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, or stenting) decompresses the main pancreatic duct and reduces pain in approximately 50-60% of patients with dominant strictures or large duct stones. Surgical drainage (Frey or Beger procedure, lateral pancreaticojejunostomy) achieves superior long-term pain control versus endoscopy in patients with dilated main duct disease and is the preferred approach in fit patients failing endoscopic therapy. Total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation (TPIAT) is reserved for end-stage, refractory pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) — lipase 40,000-80,000 units per main meal
medicalTreatments.description: Enteric-coated lipase microspheres (pancrelipase) taken with the first bite of each meal and snack to replace deficient exocrine secretion. Dose titrated based on stool frequency, fat content, and nutritional response. PPI co-prescription (omeprazole 20-40 mg daily) prevents acid inactivation of exogenous enzymes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Steatorrhoea reduced by 70-80% in clinical trials; weight gain of 2-5 kg over 3 months; fat-soluble vitamin levels normalise in 6-12 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily) for neuropathic pain
medicalTreatments.description: First-line adjuvant analgesic targeting central sensitization and visceral hypersensitivity. Reduces pain intensity and opioid requirements in randomized controlled trials for chronic pancreatitis pain. Titrated from 75 mg twice daily; common side-effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 20-30% over placebo in RCTs; allows 30-40% reduction in opioid dose when combined with structured non-opioid analgesia

medicalTreatments.name: Insulin therapy for pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes
medicalTreatments.description: Pancreatogenic diabetes requires insulin earlier than type 2 diabetes because both insulin and glucagon secretion are impaired. Hypoglycaemia risk is higher than in type 1 or type 2 diabetes due to absent glucagon counter-regulation. Basal insulin with meal-time correction is standard; SGLT-2 inhibitors are avoided due to ketoacidosis risk without adequate insulin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HbA1c target below 7-8% in most patients; intensive insulin targets require caution due to hypoglycaemia risk

medicalTreatments.name: Antioxidant supplementation (selenium, beta-carotene, vitamin C, vitamin E, methionine)
medicalTreatments.description: The ANTICIPATE trial did not show benefit in established chronic pancreatitis, but antioxidant supplements remain widely used in clinical practice for nutritional deficiency correction and as adjunct in idiopathic painful chronic pancreatitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analysis shows modest pain reduction (NRS score –0.5) vs placebo; strongest evidence in non-alcoholic idiopathic forms

medicalTreatments.name: Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) and zinc
medicalTreatments.description: Exocrine insufficiency impairs absorption of all fat-soluble vitamins; deficiencies of vitamin D (metabolic bone disease), vitamin K (coagulopathy), and vitamin A (night blindness) are clinically important. Annual monitoring with targeted replacement is standard care.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Correction of deficiencies in 8-12 weeks with appropriate supplementation; bone mineral density stabilizes with vitamin D and calcium supplementation

surgicalOptions.name: Lateral pancreaticojejunostomy (Partington-Rochelle modification)
surgicalOptions.description: The main pancreatic duct is opened along its length and drained into a Roux-en-Y jejunal limb. Indicated for dilated main duct (above 5-7 mm) without dominant inflammatory mass. Minimal pancreatic resection, lower risk of endocrine insufficiency than resectional procedures.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant pain relief in 65-80% at 2 years; superior to medical and endoscopic therapy in the ESCAPE randomized trial

surgicalOptions.name: Frey procedure (local head excision with ductal drainage)
surgicalOptions.description: Combines core decompression of the pancreatic head (site of the 'pacemaker' of pain) with lateral pancreaticojejunostomy. Superior to drainage-alone procedures in patients with inflammatory head mass or dominant head disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term pain relief in 75-85% at 5 years in multiple randomized studies; better quality-of-life outcomes than standard lateral pancreaticojejunostomy in head-dominant disease

surgicalOptions.name: Total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation (TPIAT)
surgicalOptions.description: Entire pancreas is removed; the islet cells are harvested, purified, and infused into the portal vein, where they engraft in the liver and provide insulin secretion. Reserved for end-stage refractory pain in patients without diabetes and with satisfactory islet yield.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Insulin independence achieved in 30-40% at 5 years; pain resolution in 80% regardless of insulin status; best outcomes at high-volume TPIAT centers

alternativeTreatments.name: Celiac plexus block / celiac plexus neurolysis (EUS-guided)
alternativeTreatments.description: EUS-guided injection of corticosteroid or alcohol into the celiac plexus to interrupt visceral pain signaling from the pancreas. Cochrane meta-analysis of three RCTs shows short-term pain reduction in 50-60% for 4-24 weeks; benefit not sustained beyond 6 months in most patients. Used as bridging therapy awaiting surgery or when surgical risk is prohibitive.

whySeeSpecialist: Gastroenterology specialist input is required to sub-classify chronic pancreatitis, exclude autoimmune disease and malignancy, and initiate PERT correctly. Endocrinology or diabetology consultation is needed for pancreatogenic diabetes management because treatment differs substantially from type 1 or type 2 diabetes. Referral to a high-volume pancreatic surgery center should be made when endoscopic therapy fails to control pain after 6-12 months of adequate trial.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a gastroenterologist with a dedicated pancreatic disease service who performs EUS and ERCP in-house. Surgical consultation should involve a hepatopancreaticobiliary (HPB) surgeon at a center performing at least 15-20 major pancreatic resections per year. TPIAT is available at fewer than 30 centers worldwide; referral for this procedure requires specialist network approval.
hospitalCriteria: EUS and ERCP capability for diagnostic and therapeutic ductal intervention
CT and MRI/MRCP for pancreatic imaging
Nutritional support team for malabsorption management
Pancreatic surgery service with HPB expertise
Endocrinology service for management of pancreatogenic diabetes
keyFacilities: Tertiary pancreatic disease centers with combined gastroenterology, surgery, and nutrition expertise
Advanced endoscopy units performing EUS-guided interventions and ERCP
Pain medicine clinics for opioid stewardship
preventionStrategies: Reduce alcohol intake to below 2 standard drinks per day; cessation completely is the most effective intervention for alcoholic chronic pancreatitis — it reduces pain episode frequency by 50-60% and slows structural progression.
Stop smoking entirely; cessation reduces calcification rates and lowers pancreatic cancer risk substantially in patients with chronic pancreatitis.
Treat acute pancreatitis episodes aggressively to minimize pancreatic necrosis; recurrent acute pancreatitis is a major pathway to chronic disease.
Screen first-degree relatives of patients with PRSS1-mutation hereditary pancreatitis for early symptoms and genetic status.
Correct hypertriglyceridaemia (fenofibrate, omega-3 fatty acids, dietary fat restriction) if triglycerides exceed 1,000 mg/dL to prevent recurrent acute pancreatitis converting to chronic disease.
lifestyleModifications: Complete alcohol abstinence is the most impactful modifiable intervention — studies consistently show pain episodes halve within 12 months of cessation.
Stop smoking immediately; smoking doubles the rate of pancreatic calcification and increases the 5-fold elevated cancer risk further.
Take PERT consistently with every meal and snack; skipping doses causes immediate recurrence of malabsorption symptoms.
Eat five to six small meals per day with low fat content (less than 30 g fat per meal) to minimize pancreatic secretory demand and postprandial pain.
Avoid fasting for extended periods, which destabilizes blood glucose in pancreatogenic diabetes.
Report new or worsening pain, jaundice, or unexpected weight loss promptly — annual pancreatic imaging is recommended because cancer risk is approximately 1.3-1.8% per year after 10 years of disease.

recommended: Low-fat diet (total fat below 30 g per day during malabsorptive phases) to reduce pancreatic lipase demand and steatorrhoea
Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oils (coconut oil, MCT supplements) that are absorbed without pancreatic lipase — useful calorie source in severe EPI
High-quality protein (fish, egg whites, legumes) in small, frequent servings to maintain lean mass
Fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K in supplement form due to impaired absorption; calcium 1,000-1,200 mg daily for bone protection
avoid: Alcohol completely — even small amounts trigger pain exacerbations and accelerate fibrosis
High-fat meals (above 30-40 g fat per sitting) that precipitate steatorrhoea and postprandial pain
Fatty fried foods, full-fat dairy, and high-fat processed meats that maximally stimulate pancreatic secretion
Carbonated beverages and caffeine if they provoke pain symptoms in individual patients

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (brisk walking, cycling, swimming) 30 minutes five days per week is recommended to maintain body weight, muscle mass, and bone density. Resistance training helps counter the sarcopenia that accompanies malnutrition in advanced disease. Exercise does not trigger pancreatic pain and is beneficial for pancreatogenic diabetes management. Rest during acute exacerbations; resume activity when pain is controlled.
dailyManagement: Take PERT (pancrelipase capsules) with the first bite of every meal and every snack — not before or after, as timing is critical for mixing with chyme in the duodenum.
Take the prescribed PPI 30-60 minutes before the first meal of the day to suppress gastric acid that would otherwise inactivate exogenous enzymes.
Monitor stool character daily — well-formed non-fatty stools confirm adequate enzyme replacement; return of loose or oily stools indicates under-dosing or non-adherence.
Check fasting glucose or CGM readings daily if on insulin for pancreatogenic diabetes; beware hypoglycaemia between meals.
Attend annual gastroenterology review including pancreatic imaging to screen for ductal changes and malignancy.
prognosis: Chronic pancreatitis is a lifelong condition that significantly reduces quality of life and life expectancy. Ten-year mortality is approximately 20% higher than age-matched controls, driven by pancreatic cancer (lifetime risk approximately 4-5% for non-hereditary disease, 40-55% for PRSS1 hereditary pancreatitis), cardiovascular disease, diabetes complications, and alcohol-related conditions. Continued alcohol use after diagnosis triples the rate of painful exacerbations and nearly doubles mortality risk. With comprehensive treatment — cessation of alcohol and smoking, PERT for EPI, and appropriate management of pancreatogenic diabetes — the majority of patients achieve satisfactory pain control and maintain nutrition. Surgical drainage procedures achieve meaningful pain relief in 70-85% of appropriately selected patients at 5 years. The minority with end-stage disease and refractory pain may require TPIAT, which provides pain relief in 80% even when complete insulin independence is not achieved.
recoveryTimeline: Acute exacerbations settle over 5-10 days with pain management and bowel rest. Steatorrhoea improves within 1-2 weeks of correctly dosed PERT. Weight gain with PERT is typically 2-5 kg over 3 months. Post-surgical recovery after lateral pancreaticojejunostomy or Frey procedure is 6-8 weeks. Pancreatogenic diabetes requires indefinite management.
complications: Pancreatic cancer: lifetime risk of 4-5% in non-hereditary chronic pancreatitis, rising to 40-55% in PRSS1 hereditary pancreatitis; annual CA 19-9 and pancreatic MRI/CT surveillance recommended after 10 years of disease.

Pseudocysts: fluid collections arising from disrupted ducts — many resolve spontaneously; those over 6 cm, symptomatic, or infected require endoscopic (EUS-guided cystogastrostomy), radiological, or surgical drainage.

Splenic vein thrombosis with gastric varices: occurs in 5-10% from peripancreatic inflammation; presents as upper GI bleeding from isolated gastric varices; treated with splenectomy or endoscopic variceal management.
Biliary strictures from fibrotic head tissue: progressive jaundice and cholangitis; decompressed with biliary stenting at ERCP or bilioenteric bypass surgery.

Metabolic bone disease: vitamin D deficiency and fat malabsorption cause osteoporosis in 40-50% of patients with long-standing EPI; dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) every 2 years and calcium/vitamin D supplementation are standard.
Pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes: develops in 30-50% within 20 years; carries unique hypoglycaemia risk from absent glucagon; treated with insulin and requires close endocrine follow-up.

supportResources.name: National Pancreas Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organization with information on chronic pancreatitis, disease management, clinical trials, and support groups.

supportResources.name: Pancreatic Cancer UK — Chronic Pancreatitis Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on chronic pancreatitis including diet, enzymes, and cancer surveillance guidance.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Pancreatitis
supportResources.description: ACG patient-facing overview of pancreatitis including chronic forms, enzyme therapy, and specialist management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer causes epigastric pain related to meals and H. pylori infection or NSAID use; pain is typically improved by eating (duodenal ulcer) or worsened (gastric ulcer) and responds to PPIs. Chronic pancreatitis causes persistent radiating back pain unrelieved by PPIs, with steatorrhoea, weight loss, and pancreatic calcifications on imaging.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute pancreatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute pancreatitis presents with sudden severe pain, marked lipase elevation (>3× ULN), and radiological pancreatic edema or necrosis that typically resolves within days to weeks. Chronic pancreatitis features dull persistent or episodic pain with normal or mildly elevated enzymes, pancreatic atrophy, calcifications, and irreversible exocrine and endocrine insufficiency on long-term follow-up.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pancreatic cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma presents with rapidly progressive jaundice, dramatic weight loss, a solid mass on CT with ductal obstruction and distal atrophy, and elevated CA 19-9. Chronic pancreatitis shows diffuse calcifications, a dilated irregular duct, and a clinical history of years of episodic pain; CT does not exclude cancer and EUS-guided biopsy is needed when a mass is present.

relatedConditions.name: Alcoholic liver disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both arise from sustained heavy alcohol use and frequently coexist; combined hepatic and pancreatic disease worsens nutritional status and prognosis substantially.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol use disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Alcohol use disorder is the primary modifiable cause of chronic pancreatitis; addiction treatment is integral to pancreatic disease management.

relatedConditions.name: Liver abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared gastroenterology specialty; liver involvement from portal seeding can coexist with pancreatic complications in severe abdominal disease.

faqs.question: What is chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: Chronic pancreatitis is permanent inflammation of the pancreas that destroys the tissue producing digestive enzymes and insulin. Unlike acute pancreatitis, the damage does not heal. It causes recurring abdominal pain, fatty stools from malabsorption, weight loss, and eventually diabetes. Alcohol and smoking are the leading causes in Western populations.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are persistent or recurring upper abdominal pain that bores into the back, worsened by eating and alcohol. As the disease progresses, fatty oily stools (steatorrhoea), significant weight loss, nausea, and new diabetes develop. Pain may be episodic initially but becomes constant in severe disease.

faqs.question: Can chronic pancreatitis be cured?
faqs.answer: Chronic pancreatitis cannot be reversed once established. Treatment focuses on controlling pain, replacing missing digestive enzymes, managing diabetes, and preventing complications. Alcohol and smoking cessation slow progression significantly. Surgical or endoscopic procedures relieve pain in 65-85% of selected patients but do not restore normal pancreatic function.

faqs.question: How is chronic pancreatitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: CT of the abdomen showing pancreatic calcifications, ductal dilatation, or atrophy confirms the diagnosis in advanced disease. MRI with MRCP gives superior ductal detail. Endoscopic ultrasound detects early changes. Fecal elastase-1 below 200 µg/g confirms exocrine insufficiency, and HbA1c screens for pancreatogenic diabetes.

faqs.question: Does chronic pancreatitis always require surgery?
faqs.answer: No. Most patients are managed medically with pain control, pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy, and lifestyle modification. Endoscopic therapy (ERCP with stone removal or stricture dilation) relieves pain in 50-60% with main duct obstruction. Surgery is reserved for patients failing endoscopy and offers better long-term pain control in dilated-duct disease.

faqs.question: What is pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy?
faqs.answer: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) uses enteric-coated lipase capsules (pancrelipase) taken with every meal and snack to substitute for the deficient digestive enzymes. The standard dose is 40,000-80,000 lipase units per main meal. A proton-pump inhibitor is given alongside to prevent gastric acid from inactivating the enzymes before they reach the duodenum.

faqs.question: Can you drink alcohol with chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: No. Alcohol is the most common cause of chronic pancreatitis and continuing to drink accelerates disease progression, doubles the frequency of acute pain episodes, and increases mortality. Complete abstinence reduces pain frequency by 50-60% within 12 months and substantially slows structural deterioration.

faqs.question: Does chronic pancreatitis cause diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. As islet cells are destroyed by pancreatic fibrosis, 30-50% of patients develop pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes within 10-20 years of onset. This diabetes is distinct from type 1 or type 2 because both insulin and glucagon secretion are impaired, creating a higher risk of severe hypoglycaemia requiring close management with insulin.

faqs.question: What diet is best for chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: A low-fat diet (under 30 g fat per day), small frequent meals (5-6 per day), and high-quality protein help reduce postprandial pain and malabsorption. Medium-chain triglycerides are absorbed without pancreatic enzymes and provide useful calories. Alcohol must be completely avoided. Fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K require supplementation.

faqs.question: Can chronic pancreatitis lead to cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. The lifetime risk of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma is approximately 4-5% in non-hereditary chronic pancreatitis and up to 40-55% in hereditary PRSS1-mutation pancreatitis. Annual CA 19-9 and pancreatic imaging surveillance after 10 years of disease is recommended in most guidelines to detect cancer early.

faqs.question: What causes chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: Heavy alcohol use (more than 5 drinks per day for over 5 years) causes 45-70% of cases in Western countries. Tobacco smoking is an independent cause. Genetic mutations in PRSS1, SPINK1, and CFTR genes account for 20-30% of cases. Autoimmune pancreatitis, recurrent acute pancreatitis, and obstructive causes are other recognized pathways.

faqs.question: What is steatorrhoea in chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: Steatorrhoea is fatty, oily, malodorous stools that are difficult to flush, caused by fat malabsorption when pancreatic lipase output falls below 10% of normal capacity. It develops in advanced exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and is treated with enteric-coated pancreatic enzyme replacement taken with every meal.

faqs.question: Is chronic pancreatitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: A proportion of chronic pancreatitis is genetic. Mutations in the PRSS1 gene cause autosomal-dominant hereditary pancreatitis with 80% penetrance, typically presenting in childhood. SPINK1 and CFTR mutations increase susceptibility when combined with alcohol or smoking. Genetic testing is recommended in young patients and those with family history.

faqs.question: How is pancreatogenic diabetes different from type 2 diabetes?
faqs.answer: Pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes differs from type 2 because both insulin and glucagon production are impaired. The absence of glucagon makes hypoglycaemia unpredictable and severe. Insulin is required earlier than in type 2 diabetes, and SGLT-2 inhibitors should be avoided. Treatment targets slightly higher HbA1c to reduce hypoglycaemia risk.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for chronic pancreatitis?
faqs.answer: Chronic pancreatitis is a lifelong condition with 10-year mortality approximately 20% above age-matched controls, primarily from pancreatic cancer, diabetes complications, and alcohol-related causes. With abstinence from alcohol and smoking, enzyme replacement, and pain management, most patients achieve reasonable quality of life. Continued alcohol use nearly doubles mortality risk.

metaTitle: Chronic Pancreatitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Chronic pancreatitis: irreversible pancreatic inflammation causing pain and malabsorption. Symptoms, enzyme replacement therapy, and surgical drainage reviewed.
